{"id":"da-3qn","title":"BIN database integration for card issuer identification","description":"Integrate a comprehensive BIN (Bank Identification Number) database into darkdisco for identifying card issuers from BIN prefixes found in CC dump findings.

Architecture:
1. New bin_database table: bin_prefix (varchar, indexed), bin_range_start, bin_range_end (for range-based lookups), issuer_name, card_brand (Visa/Mastercard/Amex/Discover/etc), card_type (credit/debit/prepaid), card_level (classic/gold/platinum/business), country_code, country_name, bank_url, bank_phone, source (which dataset it came from), updated_at
2. Data ingestion pipeline:
   - Parse Visa BIN range PDFs (Visa publishes BIN Table updates)
   - Parse Mastercard BIN range PDFs (Mastercard publishes similar)
   - Import from open BIN databases (binlist.net API as fallback, or static CSV datasets)
   - Management command: bd bin-import --source visa --file visa_bins.pdf
   - Management command: bd bin-import --source csv --file bins.csv
3. BIN lookup API: GET /api/bins/lookup/{prefix} returns issuer info
4. Auto-enrichment: when a finding contains BIN-matchable numbers (6-8 digit prefixes), auto-lookup and annotate the finding metadata with issuer info for all detected BINs
5. Frontend: BIN lookup tool page, and BIN issuer display in finding detail when card numbers are detected
6. PDF parsing: use pdfplumber or camelot to extract BIN range tables from Visa/MC PDFs. Handle both static prefix lists and range formats (e.g., 400000-400099 = Bank X).

Modern BIN standard is 8 digits (IIN), but legacy 6-digit prefixes still in wide use. Support both.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"crashish@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-21T18:44:56Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-03-21T18:44:56Z"}

{"notes":"Root cause analysis:

BUG 1 - Files missing from search:
_store_extracted_files() (worker.py:536) only creates ExtractedFile rows from extracted_file_contents in metadata, which only includes TEXT files (worker.py:424-434). Non-text files (images, binaries) are uploaded to S3 but get no DB row. The search endpoint queries ExtractedFile table, so non-text files are invisible.

BUG 2 - Archives showing duplicate sub-files:
/extracted-files endpoint returns individual ExtractedFile rows. Frontend loadArchives() treats each row as an 'archive' in the left panel. Multiple rows from the same archive share the same mention_id, so clicking any of them loads the same archive-contents via /mentions/{mention_id}/archive-contents. Need a proper archives-summary endpoint.

BUG 3 - Nested archives not extracted:
In-memory extraction has nested support, but _process_file_mentions only stores text file info in extracted_file_contents metadata. The extract_stored_archive backfill task correctly creates rows for ALL files but skips mentions that already have some ExtractedFile rows.

FIX PLAN:
1. Modify _process_file_mentions to store ALL extracted file metadata (not just text)
2. Modify _store_extracted_files to create rows for ALL files (matching extract_stored_archive behavior)
3. Add /archives endpoint grouping by mention_id
4. Fix frontend to use archives endpoint for left panel
5. Add reindex task for existing data"}

1. PERFORMANCE: Matching 6k mentions × 3784 watch terms takes 25+ min per cycle. This is O(n×m) brute force. Need indexing/precomputation — e.g. build a trie or inverted index from watch terms at load time, batch SQL operations, use bulk inserts.
2. RELIABILITY: Tasks were silently dying with no output. The per-mention exception handler was rolling back ALL uncommitted findings. Batch commits (every 50) were added but the core performance issue means cycles still timeout.
3. OBSERVABILITY: No progress logging during matching — 25min of silence before any output. Add periodic progress logs (every 100 mentions processed).

Goal: Matching cycle should complete in <2 minutes for 6k mentions × 4k watch terms. 7. DATA INTEGRITY: User reported cvv190_cloud files missing but found trident_cloud files still present. Verify actual data state — may not be as bad as feared but we need a clear inventory of what exists vs what's missing.

{"id":"da-s2i","title":"Parallel Telethon sessions for concurrent polling and downloading","description":"Telegram supports multiple concurrent sessions from the same account (each .session file is an independent client). Current architecture uses a single session with a Redis lock, serializing all Telegram operations.

Implementation:
1. Create multiple .session file copies from the existing authenticated session
2. Dedicated sessions: telegram_poll.session (channel reading), telegram_download_1.session and telegram_download_2.session (file downloads)
3. Poll worker uses its session to read channels — can be further split across sessions for parallel channel polling (A-M / N-Z)
4. Download workers use their sessions to download files concurrently
5. Remove the single-session Redis lock (darkdisco:telegram_session_lock) — each session is independent
6. Add flood-wait backoff per session to stay within Telegram rate limits
7. Docker-compose: mount session files as volumes, configure which session each worker type uses

Expected improvement: 2-3x throughput on polling (parallel channel reads), 3-5x on downloads (concurrent file fetches). Currently polling 337 channels takes ~4min sequentially.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-28T21:37:51Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-03-28T21:37:51Z"}
